Add.problem: Multiple images need to be combined, but blending them by hand or thinking about pixel math is slow and error-prone.
Add.replaces: Manual brightness mixing, custom shaders, or per-pixel math to add images together.
Add.thinking: Additive thinking — treating images like light or energy that increases when stacked.

Analyze.problem: You need to know what an image is doing internally instead of guessing based on how it looks.
Analyze.replaces: Eyeballing, trial-and-error, or building separate measurement systems to inspect images.
Analyze.thinking: Inspection thinking — measuring behavior before making decisions.

Anti-Alias.problem: Rendered shapes and lines look jagged and stair-stepped.
Anti-Alias.replaces: Manual smoothing tricks or higher-resolution renders just to hide rough edges.
Anti-Alias.thinking: Refinement thinking — improving visual quality after the main work is done.

Cache.problem: The same expensive image calculations are being done over and over.
Cache.replaces: Repeated re-cooking of operators or complex logic to avoid recomputation.
Cache.thinking: Memory thinking — keeping results so work does not have to be repeated.

Cache Select.problem: You need a specific frame from stored results without rebuilding timing logic.

CHOP to TOP.problem: Numerical data needs to be visualized as an image.

Composite.problem: Multiple images must be layered together using consistent rules.

Constant.problem: You need a predictable image that does not change over time.

Constant.thinking: Baseline thinking — establishing a stable, known starting point.

C++.problem: Built-in operators cannot express the logic you need.
C++.replaces: Workarounds made from many operators or limitations of fixed behavior.
C++.thinking: Construction thinking — defining behavior directly and precisely.

Render Stream In.problem: Rendered images need to be received from another process or machine.

Shared Mem Out.problem: Images need to be sent quickly to another process running on the same computer.

Edge.problem: You need to see where shapes and boundaries are in an image.

Slope.problem: Gradual changes need to be exaggerated or compressed across an image.

Inside.problem: You need to keep only the parts of an image that fall inside a shape or mask.

Outside.problem: You need to keep only the parts of an image that fall outside a shape or mask.

Over.problem: One image needs to be placed on top of another using transparency.

Projection.problem: An image needs to be cast onto geometry or another surface correctly.

Transform.problem: An image needs to be moved, rotated, or scaled in space.

Circle.problem: You need a circular shape as an image source without drawing or importing one.

Cross.problem: You need a simple cross or plus shape as a visual element or reference.

Function.problem: You need a visual representation of a mathematical function over space.

GLSL.problem: You need low-level control over how pixels are generated or changed.

GLSL Multi.problem: You need multiple GLSL operations to work together in a coordinated way.

Noise.problem: You need organic variation instead of flat or repetitive visuals.

Noise.thinking: Generative thinking — creating complexity from simple rules.

Ramp.problem: You need a smooth transition between values or colors.

Ramp.thinking: Gradient thinking — moving gradually from one state to another.

Rectangle.problem: You need a rectangular shape as a controllable image source.

Rectangle.thinking: Structural thinking — using basic shapes to define space.

Depth.problem: You need to know how far things are from the camera in a scene.

Render.problem: 3D scenes need to be turned into images.

Render Select.problem: You need access to a specific output from a render without rebuilding the render setup.

Render Simple.problem: You need a fast, straightforward render without advanced lighting or features.

Blob Track.problem: You need to detect and follow moving shapes or regions in an image.

Feedback.thinking: Temporal thinking — letting time accumulate and affect the present.

Kinect.problem: You need depth, body, or gesture data from a Kinect sensor.

Kinect.thinking: Embodied thinking — turning human presence into spatial data.

Leap Motion.problem: You need precise hand and finger tracking as input.

RealSense.problem: You need depth and tracking data from Intel RealSense cameras.

ZED.problem: You need high-quality depth, stereo vision, and tracking data.

Parameter.problem: Operator parameters need to be exposed as visual or interactive elements.

Select.problem: You need to pull specific parts from a larger image or data set.

OpenVR.problem: VR headsets and tracked devices need to drive visuals correctly.

Pack.problem: Multiple images need to be combined into a single texture efficiently.

Window.problem: You need a controllable window to display visuals on the desktop.

Sphere.replaces: Custom radial math or imported sphere geometry.
Sphere.thinking: Primitive-volume thinking — simple shapes define space.

Sprinkle.problem: Particles need to be scattered across a surface or volume.
Sprinkle.replaces: Manual point placement or uneven distributions.
Sprinkle.thinking: Distribution thinking — spreading elements by rule.

Subdivide.problem: Particle-based geometry needs more resolution or detail.
Subdivide.thinking: Refinement thinking — increasing detail without changing form.

Switch.problem: You need to choose between multiple particle inputs.
Switch.thinking: Decision thinking — one active source at a time.

Texture Map.problem: Image data needs to drive particle attributes or behavior.
Texture Map.replaces: Manual value painting or custom sampling logic.
Texture Map.thinking: Sampling thinking — behavior read from images.

TOP to.problem: Image pixels need to become particle data.
TOP to.replaces: Custom pixel-reading scripts or CPU-side extraction.
TOP to.thinking: Domain-bridging thinking — visuals converted into motion data.

Topology.problem: Particle or point connections need to be generated or analyzed.
Topology.replaces: Manual neighbor definitions or static connectivity.
Topology.thinking: Structural thinking — defining how elements are linked.

Torus.problem: You need a ring-shaped particle region or emitter.
Torus.replaces: Manual donut-shaped math or imported geometry.

Trail.problem: You need access to where particles have been over time.

Transform.problem: Particle positions need to be moved, rotated, or scaled.
Transform.thinking: Spatial adjustment thinking — repositioning without redefining behavior.

Trig.problem: You need sine, cosine, or tangent relationships in particle behavior.
Trig.thinking: Wave-based thinking — circular math drives motion.

Tube.problem: Particles need a cylindrical emission or constraint region.

Twist.problem: Particle space needs rotational deformation around an axis.
Twist.thinking: Torsion thinking — space itself is rotated.

ZED.problem: Depth and tracking data from a ZED camera needs to drive particles.
ZED.thinking: Spatial sensing thinking — particles informed by real-world depth.

Constant.problem: You need a material with a single, unchanging appearance.
Constant.replaces: Textures or lighting-based materials when flat color is enough.
Constant.thinking: Literal thinking — what you see is exactly what it is.

Depth.problem: You need depth information to affect how something is drawn.
Depth.replaces: Guessing draw order or faking depth behavior.
Depth.thinking: Spatial-awareness thinking — nearer and farther matter.

GLSL.problem: You need full control over how a surface is shaded on the GPU.
GLSL.thinking: Low-level thinking — explicitly describing how pixels are made.

In.problem: A material needs to receive inputs from elsewhere in the network.
In.replaces: Hardwired material setups or duplicated logic.
In.thinking: Access thinking — pulling appearance data from outside.

Line.problem: You need to control how line-based geometry is drawn.
Line.replaces: Default line rendering with no stylistic control.
Line.thinking: Stroke thinking — appearance defined by edges, not surfaces.

Null.problem: You need a stable reference point in a material network.
Null.replaces: Using active materials just to hold a place in the graph.

Out.problem: You need to define the final output of a material network.
Out.thinking: Boundary thinking — this is where appearance is finalized.

PBR.problem: You need materials that react realistically to light.
PBR.replaces: Hand-tuned lighting tricks and fake reflections.
PBR.thinking: Physical-model thinking — materials follow real-world rules.

Phong.problem: You need simple lighting with highlights and shading.
Phong.replaces: Flat shading when some depth and shine is needed.
Phong.thinking: Classic lighting thinking — light, normal, highlight.

Point Sprite.problem: Points need a visible, camera-facing appearance.
Point Sprite.replaces: Full geometry for tiny visual elements.
Point Sprite.thinking: Impostor thinking — minimal shape standing in for form.

Select.problem: Only part of a material network needs to be used.
Select.thinking: Extraction thinking — choosing which appearance path matters.

Switch.problem: You need to change between different material looks.
Switch.replaces: Rewiring materials or rebuilding render setups.
Switch.thinking: Decision thinking — one appearance active at a time.

Wireframe.problem: You need to render only the edges of geometry.
Wireframe.replaces: Shaded surfaces when structure needs emphasis.
Wireframe.thinking: Structural-visibility thinking — form shown through edges.

Constant.problem: You need a surface that looks the same everywhere, with no lighting or texture changes.
Constant.replaces: Complex lighting or texture setups when a flat look is enough.
Constant.thinking: Literal appearance thinking — color is not affected by the world.

Depth.problem: You need control over how depth affects what is visible.
Depth.replaces: Implicit depth handling or draw-order guesswork.
Depth.thinking: Depth-awareness thinking — near and far change how things appear.

GLSL.problem: You need custom control over how pixels are calculated on the GPU.
GLSL.replaces: Fixed material models with limited flexibility.
GLSL.thinking: Shader-thinking — explicitly defining how surfaces are drawn.

In.problem: A material needs to receive color or data from another network.
In.replaces: Self-contained materials that cannot be driven externally.
In.thinking: Input-thinking — appearance is driven by outside data.

Line.problem: You need to control how line geometry is rendered.
Line.replaces: Default line drawing with no stylistic control.
Line.thinking: Edge-focused thinking — form is defined by strokes.

Null.problem: You need an organizational placeholder inside a material network.
Null.replaces: Using active materials just to hold structure.
Null.thinking: Structural thinking — organization without visual effect.

Out.problem: You need a clear final output for a material network.
Out.thinking: Finalization thinking — this is where appearance is decided.

PBR.problem: You need materials that respond to light in a realistic way.
PBR.replaces: Hand-tuned shading tricks or non-physical lighting models.
PBR.thinking: Physical-material thinking — surfaces behave like real materials.

Phong.problem: You need simple lighting with highlights and shading.
Phong.replaces: Flat shading that lacks depth cues.
Phong.thinking: Classic-lighting thinking — light, normal, and specular response.

Point Sprite.problem: Points need to be visible as camera-facing shapes.
Point Sprite.replaces: Full geometry for tiny visual elements.
Point Sprite.thinking: Impostor thinking — minimal representation of form.

Select.problem: Only part of a material network should be used or exposed.
Select.thinking: Selection thinking — choosing the active appearance path.

Switch.problem: You need to change between different material looks.
Switch.thinking: Decision thinking — one appearance active at a time.

Wireframe.problem: You need to show only the edges of geometry.
Wireframe.replaces: Shaded surfaces when structure needs to be visible.
Wireframe.thinking: Structural-visibility thinking — form shown through edges.

Artnet.problem: Lighting control data needs to be sent or received over a network.
Artnet.replaces: Direct DMX cabling or custom network lighting code.
Artnet.thinking: Network-control thinking — lights as data on a wire.

CHOP Execute.problem: You need code to run when channel values change.
CHOP Execute.replaces: Constant polling or large script loops.
CHOP Execute.thinking: Event-based thinking — act only when something happens.

CHOP to.problem: Channel data needs to be converted into tables or text.
CHOP to.replaces: Manual copying of values or custom conversion scripts.
CHOP to.thinking: Translation thinking — signals turned into structured data.

CSN.problem: Structured control data needs to be exchanged with external systems.
CSN.replaces: Ad-hoc socket scripts or protocol glue code.
CSN.thinking: System-integration thinking — speaking a shared control language.

C++.problem: You need custom data logic that is faster or more specific than scripting.
C++.replaces: Large Python networks or workaround operators.
C++.thinking: Low-level thinking — building behavior close to the machine.

DAT Execute.problem: Scripts need to run when tables or text change.
DAT Execute.replaces: Manual script triggering or constant checks.
DAT Execute.thinking: Reactive thinking — data changes cause actions.

Error.problem: You need to see and manage errors produced by the system.
Error.replaces: Guessing why something failed or silently broke.

Etherdream.problem: Laser control data needs to be sent to Ether Dream hardware.
Etherdream.replaces: Standalone laser software or custom drivers.
Etherdream.thinking: Hardware-output thinking — data drives physical light.

Evaluate.problem: Text expressions need to be calculated into results.
Evaluate.replaces: Hardcoded values or manual math updates.
Evaluate.thinking: Expression thinking — results come from formulas.

Examine.problem: You need to inspect the contents of data tables.
Examine.replaces: Printing values to logs or guessing data shape.
Examine.thinking: Inspection thinking — looking directly at data.

FIFO.problem: You need to process data in the order it arrives.
FIFO.thinking: Queue thinking — first in, first out.

File In.problem: Text or table data needs to be loaded from disk.

File Out.problem: Data needs to be written to disk.
File Out.thinking: Archival thinking — saving results for later.

Folder.problem: You need a live list of files from a directory.
Folder.replaces: Hardcoded file paths or manual file lists.
Folder.thinking: Filesystem thinking — folders as data sources.

Info.problem: You need metadata about operators or data.
Info.thinking: Introspection thinking — the system describes itself.

Insert.problem: Data needs to be added into an existing table.
Insert.thinking: Mutation thinking — changing data in place.

JSON.problem: Structured data needs to be read or written in JSON format.
JSON.replaces: Custom parsers or flat text files.
JSON.thinking: Structured-data thinking — nested information with meaning.

Keyboard In.problem: Keyboard input needs to appear as data.
Keyboard In.thinking: Discrete-input thinking — keys as events and states.

Lookup.problem: You need to find values based on keys or indices.
Lookup.replaces: Manual searching or long conditional logic.
Lookup.thinking: Mapping thinking — keys point to meaning.

Media File In.problem: Text or metadata needs to be extracted from media files.
Media File In.replaces: External tools to read media info.

Merge.problem: Multiple tables or text streams need to be combined.
Merge.thinking: Aggregation thinking — many sources, one view.

MIDI Event.problem: You need event-based MIDI data instead of raw channels.
MIDI Event.thinking: Musical-event thinking — notes happen, then stop.

MIDI In.problem: MIDI data needs to enter the system as tables or text.

Monitors.problem: You need information about connected displays.
Monitors.replaces: Manual display configuration or guessing resolutions.
Monitors.thinking: Environment-awareness thinking — knowing the output space.

MPCDI.problem: Multi-projector calibration data needs to be loaded and used.
MPCDI.thinking: Calibration thinking — displays aligned by measured data.

MQTT Client.problem: You need to exchange messages with IoT or automation systems.
MQTT Client.replaces: Custom network protocols or polling APIs.
MQTT Client.thinking: Message-bus thinking — systems talk through topics.

Multi-Touch In.problem: Multi-touch surface input needs to be captured as data.
Multi-Touch In.thinking: Multi-point interaction thinking — many fingers, many signals.

NDI.problem: Video and metadata need to be shared over a network.
NDI.replaces: Capture cards or local-only video routing.
NDI.thinking: Network-media thinking — video as a stream.

Null.problem: You need a stable placeholder or reference in a DAT network.
Null.replaces: Using active operators just for structure.

OP Execute.problem: Scripts need to run when operators change state.
OP Execute.replaces: Manual script triggers or constant checking.
OP Execute.thinking: Reactive-system thinking — structure changes cause actions.

OP Find.problem: You need to locate operators by name, type, or pattern.
OP Find.replaces: Manually searching large networks or hardcoding operator paths.

OSC In.problem: Control messages need to arrive over OSC.
OSC In.replaces: Custom UDP parsing or proprietary control protocols.
OSC In.thinking: Open-control thinking — lightweight network messages drive behavior.

Out.problem: You need a clear output endpoint for data.
Out.thinking: Boundary thinking — defining where data leaves the system.

Panel Execute.problem: Code needs to run when UI panels are interacted with.
Panel Execute.replaces: Polling UI state or embedding logic everywhere.
Panel Execute.thinking: Interaction-driven thinking — actions follow user input.

Parameter Execute.problem: Scripts need to run when parameters change.
Parameter Execute.replaces: Manual callbacks or constant parameter checks.
Parameter Execute.thinking: Reactive thinking — parameter changes cause actions.

Par Group Execute.problem: You need to react when groups of parameters change together.
Par Group Execute.replaces: Individual parameter callbacks scattered across scripts.
Par Group Execute.thinking: Grouped-reaction thinking — changes handled as sets.

Perform.problem: The system needs to run in a locked, presentation-focused mode.
Perform.replaces: Ad-hoc fullscreen setups or manual UI hiding.

POP to.problem: Particle data needs to be converted into tables or text.
POP to.thinking: Translation thinking — simulation turned into data.

Reorder.problem: Table rows or columns need to be rearranged.
Reorder.replaces: Manual table editing or rebuilding datasets.
Reorder.thinking: Reorganization thinking — structure without changing values.

Render Pick.problem: You need to know which rendered object was selected.
Render Pick.replaces: Manual hit-testing or guessing spatial selection.

Script.problem: You need custom logic that tables alone cannot express.
Script.replaces: Large workaround networks or external tools.

Select.problem: Only specific rows, columns, or cells are needed.
Select.thinking: Extraction thinking — focusing on relevant data.

Serial.problem: Data needs to be exchanged with devices over a serial connection.
Serial.replaces: Custom serial drivers or bridge applications.

Serial Device.problem: You need information about available serial hardware.

Socket IO.problem: Real-time bidirectional messaging with web systems is needed.
Socket IO.replaces: Polling-based HTTP or custom socket handling.

SOP to.problem: Geometry data needs to become tables or text.
SOP to.replaces: Manual extraction of point or primitive data.
SOP to.thinking: Domain-bridging thinking — form converted into data.

Sort.problem: Table data needs ordering based on values.
Sort.replaces: Manual sorting or external spreadsheet tools.

Substitute.problem: Text values need to be replaced or templated.
Substitute.thinking: Pattern-replacement thinking — text as transformable data.

Switch.problem: You need to choose between multiple data sources.
Switch.thinking: Decision thinking — one active input at a time.

Table.problem: Structured data needs to be stored and edited.
Table.thinking: Tabular thinking — rows and columns carry meaning.

TCP/IP.problem: Reliable network data exchange is required.

Text.problem: Text needs to exist as editable data.
Text.thinking: Symbolic thinking — language treated as data.

Touch In.problem: Touchscreen input needs to appear as data.
Touch In.thinking: Multi-point interaction thinking — many touches, many signals.

Touch Out.problem: Data needs to drive touch-enabled interfaces.
Touch Out.thinking: Feedback thinking — data responds back to users.

Transpose.problem: Rows and columns need to be swapped.
Transpose.thinking: Perspective thinking — viewing data from another axis.

TUIO In.problem: Multi-touch and tangible input needs to be received over TUIO.
TUIO In.thinking: Surface-interaction thinking — tables and tokens as input.

UDP In.problem: Fast, low-latency network data needs to be received.

Video Devices.problem: You need information about available video input hardware.

Web Client.problem: You need to communicate with web services as a client.

Web Server.problem: You need to serve data or interfaces over the web.
Web Server.thinking: Service thinking — providing data to others.

WebRTC.problem: Low-latency audio/video streaming between peers is needed.

WebSocket.problem: Persistent, real-time communication with web clients is needed.

XML.problem: Structured hierarchical data needs to be read or written.
XML.replaces: Flat text parsing or ad-hoc formats.
